title: Measure Ingredients
summary: Measure dry and liquid ingredients accurately using common kitchen tools.
learnerPromise: You will be able to measure dry and liquid ingredients accurately using measuring cups and spoons.
whyItMatters: Accurate measuring helps your recipes turn out well and teaches you to follow instructions carefully. It is especially important in baking where ratios matter a lot.
realLifeUses: Measuring flour for a pancake recipe
Adding the right amount of rice and water to a pot
Measuring oil for a stir-fry or dressing
Following a baking recipe that calls for precise amounts
result: Accurately measured ingredients ready to use in a recipe.
youWillLearn: Use dry measuring cups for solid ingredients
Use liquid measuring cups for wet ingredients
Level off dry ingredients for accuracy
Read liquid measurements at eye level
miniChallenge: Measure one cup of flour using the spoon-and-level method, and measure half a cup of water using a liquid measuring cup at eye level.
steps: Choose the right tool: dry cups for flour, sugar, rice; liquid cups for water, milk, oil.
For dry ingredients, spoon the ingredient into the measuring cup until it is slightly heaped.
Use a flat edge (like a knife) to level off the top of the dry measuring cup.
For liquids, place the measuring cup on a flat surface and pour to the correct line.
Bend down to read the liquid at eye level to make sure it is accurate.
completionCriteria: You used the correct measuring tool for dry and liquid ingredients
Dry ingredients are leveled off evenly
Liquid is read at eye level and matches the correct measurement line
commonProblems: Scooping flour directly with the cup, which packs it too tightly
Reading a liquid measurement while holding the cup in the air
Using a dry cup for liquids, which spills easily
tips: Spoon and level dry ingredients. Do not scoop directly from the bag.
Place liquid measuring cups on the counter and bend down to read them.
When doubling a recipe, measure each ingredient separately rather than estimating.
